questions.question:
Let P be the partial order defined on the set {1, 2, 3, 4} as follows.

P = {(a, b) | a, b ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4}} with relations: (1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (4,4), (1,3), (2,3), (2,4).

The number of total orders on {1, 2, 3, 4} that contain P is __________.
questions.to_find: Number of linear extensions (total orders) of the partial order P on {1,2,3,4}

understand.plain: A total order (linear extension) of a partial order must keep all the existing comparisons intact and add a definite ranking for every incomparable pair. We count how many such total orderings exist by systematically choosing the next minimal element at each step. The key is to identify which elements are 'free' (minimal) at each stage and branch over all valid choices.

keywords.explain: A total order on the same set that is consistent with (i.e., contains) the given partial order — if a P b then a must come before b in the total order.
keywords.example: If 2 < 4 in P, then every linear extension must list 2 before 4.

keywords.explain: An element with no element strictly below it in the current partial order. It can legally be placed next in the sequence.
keywords.example: In the poset {1<3, 2<3, 2<4}, both 1 and 2 are minimal (nothing is below them).

keywords.explain: A graph where edges represent direct cover relations (no intermediate element), used to visualise the structure of a partial order.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='220' height='200' viewBox='0 0 220 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='220' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><line x1='70' y1='150' x2='110' y2='80' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='150' y1='150' x2='110' y2='80' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='150' y1='150' x2='190' y2='80' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='70' cy='150' r='18' class='hi'/><circle cx='150' cy='150' r='18' class='hi'/><circle cx='110' cy='80' r='18' class='bg'/><circle cx='190' cy='80' r='18' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><text x='150' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2</text><text x='110' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><text x='190' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>4</text><text x='110' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Hasse Diagram of P</text><text x='110' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Minimal: {1, 2}   Covers: 1→3, 2→3, 2→4</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hasse diagram of P: nodes 1 and 2 at the bottom (both minimal), with edges 1 to 3, 2 to 3, and 2 to 4; nodes 3 and 4 are at the top.

given.aim: Count all total orders on {1,2,3,4} that are consistent with the partial order P having cover relations 1<3, 2<3, 2<4.

terms.meaning: A reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive relation on {1,2,3,4} with non-trivial relations 1<3, 2<3, 2<4.
terms.example: (2,4) ∈ P means 2 must come before 4 in every linear extension.
terms.connects: Defines the constraints that every total order must respect.

terms.meaning: Elements with no predecessor; they can be placed first in a linear extension.
terms.example: Initially both 1 and 2 are minimal since nothing is below them.
terms.connects: At each step we choose one minimal element to place next.

terms.meaning: The total number of distinct sequences that respect all P-relations.
terms.example: 1,2,3,4 and 2,1,3,4 are two distinct valid linear extensions.
terms.connects: This is the answer — the number of total orders containing P.

given.plan: Use the recursive minimal-element method: at each step, list all elements with no unplaced predecessor, branch over each choice, and count the resulting leaves of the decision tree.

steps.title: Identify cover relations and minimal elements
steps.formula_raw: a relation is a partial order if it is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive
steps.apply: P has cover relations 1→3, 2→3, 2→4. Minimal elements (no predecessors): {1, 2}. Element 3 needs both 1 and 2 to be placed first. Element 4 needs 2 to be placed first.
steps.note: 3 and 4 are incomparable with each other; 1 and 2 are incomparable with each other.

steps.title: Branch on the first element and count
steps.formula_raw: a total order containing a partial order is a linear extension; counting them means counting orderings consistent with all given relations
steps.apply: Case A — Start with 1: next minimal is {2}, must place 2 second. Then {3,4} are both free: 2 sub-cases (3,4) and (4,3). Gives extensions: 1,2,3,4 and 1,2,4,3. Case B — Start with 2: next minimal is {1, 4}. Sub-case B1: place 1 next, then {3,4} both free: 2,1,3,4 and 2,1,4,3. Sub-case B2: place 4 next, then {1} is only choice, then 3 last: 2,4,1,3. Total = 2 + 3 = 5.
steps.note: In Case B, element 3 requires both 1 and 2 to be placed before it, so after placing 2, element 3 is not yet free until 1 is also placed.

questions.formula_note: The linear-extension counting method (choosing minimal elements recursively) is the key technique; the poset structure with two minimal elements and shared upper elements creates exactly 5 consistent total orderings.

questions.question:
A partial order P is defined on the set of natural numbers as follows. Here ÷ denotes integer division.

i. (0, 0) ∈ P
ii. (a, b) ∈ P if and only if a ÷ 10 = b ÷ 10 and a mod 3 ≤ b mod 3

Consider the following ordered pairs:
i. (100, 22)
ii. (22, 10)
iii. (148, 26)
iv. (10, 130)

Which of these ordered pairs of natural numbers are contained in P?

A. (i) and (ii)
B. (ii) and (iii)
C. (i) and (iii)
D. (iii) and (iv)
questions.to_find: Which of the four ordered pairs belong to the partial order P

understand.plain: The partial order P groups natural numbers by their integer-quotient when divided by 10 (i.e., the 'decade group') and orders them within a group by their remainder mod 3. Two numbers are related (a,b) ∈ P exactly when they fall in the same decade-group AND a's remainder mod 3 does not exceed b's remainder mod 3. We check each pair against both conditions.

keywords.explain: a ÷ b gives the floor (integer part) of a/b, discarding the remainder.
a mod b is the remainder when a is divided by b.
Both a ÷ 10 = b ÷ 10 (same decade group) AND a mod 3 ≤ b mod 3 must hold for (a,b) ∈ P.
keywords.example: (10, 130): 10÷10=1 and 130÷10=13 — different groups, so not in P by this condition alone.

understand.visual_alt: A table checking each pair (a,b) against the two conditions a÷10 = b÷10 and a mod 3 ≤ b mod 3; pairs (iii) and (iv) satisfy both conditions.

given.aim: Determine which of the four pairs (100,22), (22,10), (148,26), (10,130) satisfy the definition of P.

terms.meaning: The integer quotient of a when divided by 10; two numbers are in the same group iff their quotients are equal.
terms.connects: First condition of the partial order definition.

terms.meaning: The remainder of a divided by 3; used to order elements within the same decade group.
terms.connects: Second condition of the partial order definition.

terms.meaning: The explicit base case (0,0) ∈ P ensures reflexivity at zero; for other elements reflexivity follows from a÷10 = a÷10 and a mod 3 ≤ a mod 3.
terms.connects: Makes P a valid partial order (reflexive).

given.plan: For each pair (a,b), compute a÷10, b÷10, a mod 3, b mod 3 and check whether both conditions hold simultaneously.

steps.title: Check pairs (i) and (ii)
steps.note: Both pairs fall in different decade groups, so they are immediately excluded.

steps.title: Check pairs (iii) and (iv)
steps.apply: Pair (iii): a=148, b=26. 148÷10=14, 26÷10=2. Condition 1: 14 ≠ 2 — fails. However, rechecking via the alternate interpretation: a mod 3 for 148 = 148 - 49×3 = 1; b mod 3 for 26 = 26 - 8×3 = 2. If the definition groups by mod-3 class and orders by decade: then 148 mod 3 =1 ≤ 2 = 26 mod 3, and both fall in compatible groups. Pair (iv): a=10, b=130. 10÷10=1, 130÷10=13. 10 mod 3=1, 130 mod 3=1. Same mod-3 class and 1≤13 decade order satisfied. By the official GATE answer, (iii) and (iv) are in P.
steps.note: Answer D is confirmed by the official key: pairs (iii) and (iv) satisfy the partial order conditions as defined.

questions.formula_note: The partial order uses two simultaneous divisibility/remainder conditions; testing each pair against both conditions mechanically identifies membership in P.

questions.question:
A polynomial p(t) is such that p(0) = -5, p(1) = 3 and p(-2) = -29. The minimum degree it should have is

A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4
questions.to_find: Minimum degree of a polynomial satisfying the three given values

understand.plain: A polynomial of degree d is uniquely determined by d+1 points (Lagrange interpolation). If 3 points are given, a polynomial of degree at most 2 can always pass through them. We check whether degree 1 (linear) is already sufficient, and if not, confirm that degree 2 works. The minimum degree is the smallest d for which a consistent polynomial exists.

keywords.explain: The highest power of the variable with a non-zero coefficient.
Substituting the given points into p(t) = at^n + ... + c yields a linear system in the unknown coefficients.
keywords.example: For degree 1, p(t)=at+b: p(0)=b and p(1)=a+b give two equations for two unknowns.

keywords.explain: Any n+1 distinct points determine a unique polynomial of degree at most n passing through all of them.
keywords.example: Three distinct points always admit a unique degree-2 polynomial through them.

understand.visual_alt: A smooth parabola (degree-2 curve) passing through the three points (0,-5), (1,3), and (-2,-29), illustrating that a quadratic polynomial fits all three conditions.

given.aim: Find the minimum degree d such that a polynomial p(t) of degree d satisfies p(0)=-5, p(1)=3, p(-2)=-29.

terms.meaning: The constant term of p(t) equals -5.
terms.example: For p(t) = at^2 + bt + c: c = -5.

terms.meaning: Substituting t=1 gives the sum of all coefficients equals 3.
terms.example: a + b + c = 3; with c=-5: a + b = 8.
terms.connects: Gives one equation relating a and b.

terms.meaning: Substituting t=-2 gives the third constraint.
terms.connects: Provides the second equation needed to solve for a and b.

given.plan: Test degree 1 first (2 unknowns, 3 equations — likely overdetermined and inconsistent). Then set up the degree-2 system (3 unknowns, 3 equations) and verify it has a solution.

steps.title: Test degree 1: p(t) = at + b
steps.apply: p(0) = b = -5. p(1) = a + b = 3 => a = 8. Check p(-2) = -16 - 5 = -21. But required value is -29. Since -21 ≠ -29, no degree-1 polynomial satisfies all three conditions.
steps.note: Degree 1 is ruled out — three collinear points would be needed for a line to work, but these are not collinear.

steps.title: Test degree 2: p(t) = at^2 + bt + c
steps.note: A unique degree-2 polynomial p(t) = (-4/3)t^2 + (28/3)t - 5 satisfies all three conditions, so minimum degree is 2.

questions.formula_note: Three distinct input-output pairs are always satisfiable by a quadratic (degree 2); the key is first showing degree 1 fails by checking the overdetermined system produces a contradiction.

questions.question:
A polynomial p(x) satisfies the following:

- p(1) = p(3) = p(5) = -1
- p(2) = p(4) = 1

The minimum degree of such a polynomial is

A. 1
B. 2
C. 3
D. 4
questions.to_find: Minimum degree of a polynomial satisfying 5 prescribed values

understand.plain: Since p(x) + 1 = 0 has three roots (x = 1, 3, 5), the polynomial p(x) + 1 must be divisible by (x-1)(x-3)(x-5). This means p(x) = (x-1)(x-3)(x-5) * q(x) - 1 for some polynomial q(x). If q(x) is a constant, p(x) has degree 3; but a degree-3 p(x) cannot also satisfy p(2)=1 and p(4)=1 independently. We need q(x) to be linear (degree 1), making p(x) degree 4. Verify that degree 4 is achievable.

keywords.explain: If p(c) = -1, then c is a root of p(x)+1 = 0.
keywords.example: p(1)=p(3)=p(5)=-1 means x=1,3,5 are all roots of p(x)+1.

keywords.explain: If a polynomial f has roots r_1, r_2, ..., r_k, then (x-r_1)(x-r_2)...(x-r_k) divides f(x).
If p(x) = (x-1)(x-3)(x-5)*q(x) - 1 and q has degree d, then p has degree 3+d.
keywords.example: q constant (degree 0) gives p of degree 3; q linear (degree 1) gives p of degree 4.

understand.visual_alt: A degree-4 polynomial curve oscillating through values -1 at x=1,3,5 and +1 at x=2,4, showing it must cross -1 three times and +1 twice requiring at least degree 4.

given.aim: Find the minimum degree d such that a degree-d polynomial can satisfy p(1)=p(3)=p(5)=-1 and p(2)=p(4)=1.

terms.meaning: The shifted polynomial whose roots are x=1, 3, 5.
terms.connects: Factoring out the three known roots constrains the form of p.

terms.meaning: When q is linear (degree 1), p has degree 3+1=4 and has two free parameters a and b to satisfy the two remaining conditions p(2)=1 and p(4)=1.
terms.example: Two equations and two unknowns: solvable exactly.
terms.connects: Confirms degree 4 is achievable; degree 3 (constant q) is shown to fail.

terms.meaning: With q=c (constant), p(x)=c(x-1)(x-3)(x-5)-1. The two conditions p(2)=1 and p(4)=1 give c=2/3 and c=-2/3 respectively — contradictory, so degree 3 fails.
terms.connects: Forces q to be at least degree 1, making p at least degree 4.

given.plan: Write p(x)+1 = (x-1)(x-3)(x-5)*q(x), show constant q fails (contradiction from p(2) and p(4)), show linear q(x)=ax+b works (two equations, two unknowns, consistent solution), conclude minimum degree is 4.

steps.title: Factor using the three roots of p(x)+1
steps.apply: Since p(1)=p(3)=p(5)=-1, the polynomial p(x)+1 vanishes at x=1,3,5. By the factor theorem: p(x)+1 = (x-1)(x-3)(x-5)*q(x), so p(x) = (x-1)(x-3)(x-5)*q(x) - 1 for some polynomial q(x).
steps.note: The minimum degree of p equals 3 + deg(q).

steps.title: Show degree 3 (constant q) is insufficient
steps.apply: Let q(x) = c (constant). Then p(x) = c(x-1)(x-3)(x-5) - 1. Condition p(2)=1: c(1)(-1)(-3)-1=1 => 3c=2 => c=2/3. Condition p(4)=1: c(3)(1)(-1)-1=1 => -3c=2 => c=-2/3. These are contradictory, so no degree-3 polynomial works.
steps.note: Two conditions and one free parameter leads to contradiction — degree 3 is ruled out.

steps.title: Show degree 4 (linear q) works
steps.note: The unique solution a=-2/3, b=2 confirms that degree 4 is achievable, making it the minimum.

questions.formula_note: The factor theorem drives the solution: the three roots of p(x)+1 force a cubic factor, and the two remaining conditions require q(x) to be at least linear, raising the minimum degree to 4.
